are much more sensitive. This imaging can be 
postponed in cases of typical UC, based on endoscopy 
and histology. It remains particularly important in 
cases of suspected Crohn’s disease, in patients whose 
ileum could not be visualized, in patients presenting 
with apparent ulcerative colitis with an atypical pre-
sentation and in patients with indeterminate colitis. As 
in adults, capsule endoscopy has an elective indication in 
cases of predominant or isolated small bowel involvement, and to facilitate the differential 
diagnosis between CD and UC (40). Children are most 
often able to swallow the capsule endoscope from the age 
of 8–10 years. It can be deployed endoscopically from 
10 kg.
Endoscopy and primary lesions
Systematic esophagogastroduodenoscopy and ileo-
colonoscopy are recommended. Even 
in the absence of macroscopic lesions of the upper digestive 
tract, performing multiple, segmental biopsies (2 per 
segment) allows histopathology to demonstrate the presence of gr
